narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 58% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile includes a first-year school decile of 1, meaning your grades placed you at the bottom of your class that year, and a third-year decile of 2, still quite low compared to the typical student's mid-range performance around the 5th or 6th decile. Your LSAT score of 24 is well below the average of about 37, your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 falls short of the usual 3.2, and your lower-middle family income is on the lower side of what's common. As a Black woman planning to study full-time—which is the norm for most applicants—these details all factored into the model's view of your bar exam readiness.

The most influential factor by far was your third-year decile, which strongly signaled a higher risk of bar failure compared to stronger performers. Next in importance was your first-year decile, which also pushed the prediction toward failure due to its low ranking. Your LSAT score ranked third in influence, reinforcing concerns because it's significantly below what successful bar takers typically score. Family income came fourth, with your lower-middle bracket contributing to the risk assessment in line with historical data. Your racial background as Black was fifth, reflecting underrepresentation and related outcomes in the dataset.

Your undergraduate GPA placed sixth, below average and adding to the failure likelihood, while full-time status and gender had the least sway, both nudging slightly toward higher risk but not decisively. Overall, every aspect of your profile pulled the prediction away from bar passage, with no standout strengths to counterbalance the weaker areas. Compared to typical admitted students, who boast higher deciles, LSATs around 37, and GPAs over 3.2, your metrics align more closely with those who struggled on the bar.

To improve your chances next time, focus on retaking the LSAT—many students boost their scores significantly with targeted prep, and it's one of the most changeable factors. Building stronger academic habits could help if you pursue further coursework or a post-bac program to lift your record beyond those low deciles. While background elements like income or demographics are harder to shift, excelling in these academic metrics has turned around predictions for others in similar situations. Keep pushing; with focused effort, you can reshape this story.
